name: Colombian Arepas Rellenas
steps: Mix the corn flour with warm water and a pinch of salt into a soft, pliable dough; knead in half the cheese.
Shape into a flat disc and cook in a lightly oiled skillet over medium heat, 5-6 minutes per side, until golden with dark spots.
Split while hot, butter, and stuff with the remaining cheese.
steps_detailed: Mix the corn flour with warm water and a pinch of salt, kneading until you have a soft, smooth, non-sticky dough - add water gradually since the exact amount varies.
Knead half the crumbled queso fresco into the dough.
Divide into a ball and flatten into a disc about 1/2 inch thick.
Cook in a lightly oiled heavy skillet over medium heat, 5-7 minutes per side, until golden brown with some dark charred spots and cooked through.
While still hot, split it most of the way open like a pita, butter the inside, and stuff generously with the remaining queso fresco so it melts slightly.

name: Colombian Changua
steps: Bring the milk and a splash of water to a gentle simmer with the onion.
Crack the egg directly into the simmering milk and poach 3-4 minutes until the white is set.
Ladle into a bowl and top with the cilantro.
steps_detailed: Combine the milk, 1/2 cup water, onion, and garlic in a small pot and bring to a gentle simmer.
Crack the egg directly into the simmering liquid and poach gently, 3-4 minutes, until the white is fully set but the yolk is still soft.
Ladle the warm milk broth and egg into a bowl.
Top with the chopped cilantro and serve with torn pieces of bread or tortilla for dipping - the classic Bogota breakfast, especially good on a cold morning.

name: Colombian Calentado Paisa
steps: Fry the cooked rice and beans together in the butter until heated through and slightly crisp in spots.
Fry the egg separately to your liking.
Serve the rice and beans topped with the fried egg.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion in half the butter until soft.
Add the cooked rice and pinto beans, and fry together over medium-high heat, pressing occasionally, until heated through with some crispy, golden bits.
In a separate pan, fry the egg in the remaining butter to your preferred doneness.
Plate the rice and beans topped with the fried egg, with the sliced avocado alongside - a classic way to use up last night's leftovers.

name: Colombian Ajiaco Bogotano
steps: Simmer the chicken breast in water 20 minutes until cooked through; shred and return to the pot.
Add the potato and simmer 15-18 minutes until it partially breaks down and thickens the broth.
Add the corn and simmer 5 more minutes, then serve topped with the sour cream.
steps_detailed: Combine the chicken breast, onion, garlic, and chicken broth plus 1.5 cups water in a pot. Simmer 20-22 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Remove the chicken, shred it, and return it to the pot.
Add the potato and simmer 18-20 minutes, stirring occasionally and mashing some of the potato against the pot, until the broth thickens naturally.
Add the corn and simmer 5-8 more minutes.
Ladle into bowls and top each with a spoonful of sour cream, a few capers, and sliced avocado - the traditional trio of toppings.

name: Colombian Bandeja Paisa
steps: Brown the beef and season well.
Warm the beans and cook the rice separately.
Fry the plantain slices until golden, and plate everything together.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion until soft, then brown the ground beef with it, seasoning well with salt.
Warm the pinto beans in a separate pot, and cook the rice according to package directions.
Fry the plantain slices in the oil until golden on both sides, then fry the egg in the same pan.
Arrange the beef, beans, rice, fried plantain, fried egg, and sliced avocado all together on one large plate - this is meant to be a generous, varied platter.

name: Colombian Sancocho
steps: Simmer the chicken in water 20 minutes.
Add the cassava and plantain and simmer 15 minutes, then add the corn and simmer 5 more minutes.
Season and serve hot with rice on the side.
steps_detailed: Combine the chicken, onion, garlic, and bay leaf with 2.5 cups water in a pot. Simmer 20 minutes.
Add the cassava and plantain, and simmer 15-18 minutes until tender.
Add the corn and simmer 5-8 more minutes.
Remove the bay leaf, stir in the cilantro, and serve hot in bowls with rice alongside.

name: Colombian Frijoles Antioquenos
steps: Saute the onion until soft, then add the beans and tomatoes with a splash of their liquid.
Simmer 15 minutes, mashing some of the beans to thicken.
Serve with the fried plantain alongside.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, and bell pepper until soft, 5-6 minutes.
Add the pinto beans and diced tomatoes along with a splash of the bean liquid, and simmer 15-18 minutes.
Mash about a quarter of the beans against the side of the pot to naturally thicken the stew.
Stir in the cilantro and serve with the fried plantain alongside.

name: Colombian Sobrebarriga

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Simmer the beef in the broth with the onion for 45-50 minutes until very tender; shred or slice.
Simmer the shredded beef in the diced tomatoes for 10 minutes to finish.
Serve with rice and the braising liquid spooned over.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Combine the beef, onion, garlic, bay leaf, and beef broth in a pot, adding water if needed to just cover the meat.
Simmer, partially covered, 45-55 minutes until the beef is fork-tender.
Remove the beef, shred or slice it thickly against the grain, and set aside. Strain and reserve the braising liquid.
Combine the beef with the diced tomatoes and about 1/2 cup of the reserved braising liquid, and simmer 10-12 minutes to finish and thicken slightly.
Stir in the cilantro and serve with rice, spooning extra braising liquid over the top.

name: Colombian Pollo Sudado
steps: Brown the chicken thigh, then remove and set aside.
Saute the onion, add the tomatoes and potato, and simmer 5 minutes.
Return the chicken and simmer covered 20-25 minutes until the chicken and potato are both tender.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken and brown it in a pot over medium-high heat, 4-5 minutes per side, then remove and set aside.
In the same pot, saute the onion, garlic, and bell pepper for 5-6 minutes until softened.
Add the diced tomatoes, cumin, and potato, and simmer 5 minutes.
Return the chicken to the pot, add a splash of water if needed, cover, and simmer over low heat 22-25 minutes until the chicken and potato are both tender.
Stir in the cilantro before serving.

name: Colombian Lechona-Style Pulled Pork
steps: Season the pork well and roast or braise until completely tender and easily shredded, about 1.5-2 hours covered at 325F.
Shred the meat and toss with the pan juices and onion.
Serve with the shredded cabbage and a squeeze of lime.
steps_detailed: Season the pork shoulder generously with salt, cumin, oregano, and half the garlic. Let sit at least 15 minutes, or overnight in the fridge for deeper flavor.
Place in a covered baking dish with the onion and remaining garlic, and roast at 325F (165C) for 1.5-2 hours until completely tender and shreds easily with a fork.
Shred the meat, discarding excess fat, and toss with the pan juices to keep it moist.
Serve with rice, the shredded cabbage, and a good squeeze of lime juice - traditionally lechona is a whole roasted stuffed pig, but this captures the same slow-cooked, well-seasoned pork.

name: Colombian Beef and Vegetable Mondongo-Style Stew

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Simmer the beef in water 35-40 minutes until tender.
Add the hominy, potato, and carrot, and simmer 15-18 minutes more until the vegetables are tender.
Season and serve hot in bowls.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Combine the beef, onion, garlic, and cumin with 2.5 cups water in a pot. Simmer 35-40 minutes until the beef is tender.
Add the hominy, potato, and carrot, and simmer 15-20 more minutes until the vegetables are tender and the broth has thickened slightly.
Stir in the cilantro and serve hot in wide bowls with rice.

name: Colombian Empanadas
steps: Mix the corn flour with warm water and a pinch of salt into a soft dough.
Brown the beef with the onion, season well, and let cool.
Shape the dough into discs, fill with the beef, fold and seal, then fry until golden and crisp.
steps_detailed: Mix the corn flour with warm water and a pinch of salt, kneading until a smooth, pliable dough forms.
Saute the onion and garlic, then add the ground beef and cumin, browning fully, 6-8 minutes. Stir in the diced boiled potato and let the filling cool.
Flatten small balls of dough into thin discs, place a spoonful of filling in the center, and fold into a half-moon, pressing the edges to seal well.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the empanadas 2-3 minutes per side until deep golden and crisp.
Drain on a paper towel and serve hot.

name: Colombian Arepas de Choclo
steps: Blend the corn kernels coarsely, then mix with the corn flour, cheese, and sugar.
Shape into small discs and cook in a lightly oiled skillet over medium heat, 5 minutes per side, until golden.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Pulse the corn kernels in a food processor until coarsely ground - some texture is good.
Mix with the corn flour, crumbled queso fresco, sugar, and melted butter until a thick, cohesive batter forms.
Shape into small discs about 1/2 inch thick.
Cook in a lightly oiled skillet over medium heat, 5-6 minutes per side, until golden brown and cooked through.
Serve warm - these are naturally a bit sweet from the corn and sugar, balanced by the salty cheese.

name: Colombian Bunuelos
steps: Mix the cassava starch, cheese, and egg into a soft dough.
Roll into small balls.
Fry in the oil, turning often, until puffed and deep golden all over, about 5-6 minutes total.
steps_detailed: Combine the cassava starch, crumbled queso fresco, egg, and baking powder into a soft, slightly sticky dough.
Roll into balls about 1.5 inches across.
Heat the oil in a deep skillet over medium heat. Fry the balls, turning frequently so they cook evenly and puff into a round shape, for 5-7 minutes until deep golden and cooked through in the center.
Drain on a paper towel and serve warm.

name: Colombian Obleas con Arequipe

ingredients.note: for thin wafers, or use store-bought wafers

steps: If making wafers from scratch, mix the flour with water into a thin batter and cook in a hot, lightly greased pan until paper-thin and crisp; store-bought wafers work well too.
Spread a generous layer of dulce de leche between two wafers.
Sprinkle with the crumbled cheese for the classic sweet-salty combination.
steps_detailed: Using thin, crisp wafers (store-bought oblea rounds are traditional and easiest), spread a generous, even layer of dulce de leche over one wafer.
Sprinkle the crumbled queso fresco over the dulce de leche.
Add a light drizzle of sweetened condensed milk if you'd like extra sweetness, then top with a second wafer, pressing gently.
Serve immediately - obleas are meant to be eaten right after assembling, while the wafer is still crisp.

name: Colombian Brevas con Arequipe
steps: Simmer the dried figs in the milk with a splash of water for 15-20 minutes until plump and tender.
Split the softened figs and fill with the dulce de leche.
Serve warm or at room temperature.
steps_detailed: Combine the dried figs, milk, cinnamon, and sugar with 1/4 cup water in a small saucepan.
Simmer gently for 20-25 minutes until the figs are very plump and tender and the liquid has reduced to a light syrup.
Let cool slightly, then split each fig open and fill with a spoonful of dulce de leche.
Drizzle any remaining syrup over the top before serving.

name: Colombian Flan de Panela
steps: Melt half the panela in a small dish over low heat to form a thin caramel layer on the bottom.
Whisk the milk, egg, remaining panela, and vanilla together, then pour over the caramel.
Bake in a water bath at 325F for 35-40 minutes until just set, then chill and unmold before serving.
steps_detailed: In a small baking dish, melt about a third of the panela over low heat (or in the oven briefly) until it becomes a thin, syrupy caramel coating the bottom.
Whisk together the milk, eggs, remaining panela, and vanilla until the panela is fully dissolved.
Pour the custard mixture over the caramel layer in the dish.
Set the dish into a larger pan with hot water reaching halfway up the sides, and bake at 325F (165C) for 35-45 minutes until just set with a gentle wobble.
Chill thoroughly, at least 2 hours, then run a knife around the edge and invert onto a plate to unmold, letting the caramel run down over the top.

name: Colombian Tres Leches
steps: Beat the eggs and sugar until pale, then fold in the flour to make a simple sponge batter.
Bake at 350F for 18-20 minutes until springy and golden, then let cool and poke holes all over.
Whisk the three milks together and pour slowly over the cooled cake so it soaks in fully.
steps_detailed: Beat the eggs and sugar together until pale and thickened, then beat in the vanilla.
Sift the flour and baking powder together and fold gently into the egg mixture until just combined.
Pour into a small buttered baking dish and bake at 350F (175C) for 18-22 minutes until springy to the touch and golden.
Let the cake cool completely, then poke holes all over the surface with a fork or skewer.
Whisk together the condensed milk, evaporated milk, and whole milk, and slowly pour over the cake, letting it soak in fully - it should look quite saturated.
Chill at least an hour, then top with the whipped sour cream (standing in for whipped cream) before serving.

name: Colombian Hogao
steps: Saute the onion in the oil until very soft.
Add the tomatoes and cumin and simmer 10-12 minutes until thickened into a rich, jammy sauce.
Use as a base for rice, beans, or eggs.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, bell pepper, and garlic in the oil over medium-low heat for 8-10 minutes until very soft and starting to caramelize.
Add the diced tomatoes and cumin, and simmer 12-15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until thickened into a rich, jammy sauce.
Stir in the cilantro just before using.
This is the flavor base for countless Colombian dishes - stirred into rice, spooned over beans, or served alongside eggs.

name: Colombian Aji Picante
steps: Combine the jalapeno, onion, and cilantro in a small bowl.
Stir in the vinegar and a splash of water, and season with salt.
Let sit at least 15 minutes before serving alongside empanadas or arepas.
steps_detailed: Finely mince the jalapeno and combine with the onion and cilantro in a small bowl.
Stir in the vinegar, lime juice, and a splash of water, and season generously with salt.
Let sit at room temperature at least 20 minutes so the onion softens and the flavors meld.
Serve as the classic table condiment alongside empanadas, arepas, or just about anything.
